mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage policies as code for security, compliance, and governance. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies consistently across their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include the need for a learning curve in policy definition and potential performance impacts depending on the complexity of the policies.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install Kyverno using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace with the specified version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Creates a namespace for Kyverno.
> Installs Kyverno in the specified namespace.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify they are running.

steps.title: Create a sample policy
steps.description:
This command creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-label
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-label
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> Applies a sample policy that requires a label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-label
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-label
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy
steps.description:
This command checks if the policy is applied correctly and ready.

```bash

kubectl get cpol require-label -o yaml -n kyverno

```

> Displays the details of the created policy.
commands: kubectl get cpol require-label -o yaml -n kyverno

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno from the cluster. Be aware that this will delete all policies and associated resources.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls the Kyverno release.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > kyverno-policies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing policies to a file.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the new pods are running.
commands: kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > kyverno-policies-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to validate resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the resource being created does not comply with the defined policies.

**Fix:**
Check the policy definitions and ensure the resource matches the required patterns.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the policy or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' for errors and adjust resources or policies accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may not be applied correctly or is in an invalid state.

**Fix:**
Verify the policy status with 'kubectl get cpol -n kyverno' and check for any errors.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to create resources due to validation errors
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The resource does not meet the validation criteria set in the policies.

**Fix:**
Review the policy rules and ensure that the resource definitions comply with the required patterns.

versionNotes.changes: Improved policy validation performance, added support for new Kubernetes resource types.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the 'validate' field in favor of 'validatePattern'.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.16.x, ensure to update your policies to use 'validatePattern'.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-label
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-label
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF
kubectl get cpol require-label -o yaml -n kyverno

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and Helm installed.
